description: Fig. 1. Atomic configurations and GB structures of nanocrystalline samples with Schwarz crystals. (a) Atomic configuration of simulated sample with $d = 4 . 6 7 \ \mathrm { n m }$ after equilibration. The FCC atoms are transparent, and the GBs are replaced by a translucent surface. The part in the yellow box represents a unit cell. (b) Full-atom configuration of unit cell. (c) Atomic configurations of GB and CTB networks in the unit cell. (d) Curved surface representation of GB structure of unit cell. This curved GB resembles the Schwarz D-surface described by Eq. (5).

Fig. 2. Thermal stability of simulated samples. (a) Variation in potential energy per atom with heating temperature in Schwarz crystals. (b) Critical temperature $T _ { \mathrm { c } }$ normalized by the melting temperature as a function of grain size. (c), (d) Evolution of fractions of FCC, GB and CTB atoms during heating of simulated samples with d $= 3 . 0 4 $ and $4 . 6 7 \ \mathrm { n m }$ . Insets show two snapshots of simulated samples before and after grain coarsening or melting.

Fig. 3. Tensile stress strain curves and calculated strength of simulated samples with different grain sizes. (a) Tensile stress strain curves of Schwarz crystals with different grain sizes. (b) Variation in tensile strength with grain size. The red dashed line is obtained by fitting our simulation data with the Hall Petch equation, while the black solid line is from previous experiments [18].

Fig. 4. Deformation behaviors and mechanisms of Schwarz crystal. (a) (c) A sequence of snapshots of simulated sample with $d = 3 . 8 2 \mathrm { n m }$ under different strains. (d) (f) Dislocation structures in simulated sample with $d = 3 . 8 2 \ \mathrm { n m }$ during plastic deformation. (g) (i) A sequence of snapshots of simulated sample with $d = 1 2 . 3 0 \mathrm { n m }$ under different strains. (j) (l) Dislocation structures in simulated sample with $d =$ 12.30 nm during plastic deformation. (m) (o) Annihilation of curved GBs due to detwinning in the simulated sample with $d = 3 . 8 2 \mathrm { n m }$ . The right pictures represent the rear view of the left pictures. The dashed lines in the lower pictures correspond to the CTBs shown in the upper pictures. (b) （c）

Fig. 5. Comparison between GB-mediated processes of Schwarz and Wigner Seitz nanocrystals. (a) Variation in the volume fraction of GBs with the grain size. The data points are from our MD simulations. Red and green dashed lines are calculated by multiplying the area of the Schwarz D-surface or CTB with GB width $( \sim 1 \mathrm { n m } )$ or CTB width $( 0 . 2 1 \ \mathrm { n m } )$ , respectively, and then dividing by the overall volume of Schwarz crystal. (b) ${ < } D _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ^ { 2 } { > }$ of two kinds of crystals with different grain sizes as a function of the applied strain. (c) Dependence of GB diffusivity of two kinds of crystals with $d = 8 . 5 6 \ \mathrm { n m }$ on the temperature and applied stress. $D ^ { \mathrm { r e f } } \left( 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } / s \right)$ ) is used to ensure that $D _ { \mathrm { G B } } / D ^ { \mathrm { r e f } }$ is dimensionless.
